question.text: Which type of investor account can an NRI use to invest in mutual funds in India?

answer.text: Any overseas bank account with SWIFT facility

explanation.text: **The Logic:** NRIs can invest in Indian mutual funds through either NRE (Non-Resident External) or NRO (Non-Resident Ordinary) bank accounts held with authorized dealer banks in India. NRE accounts hold foreign earnings remitted to India (fully repatriable), while NRO accounts hold Indian income (restricted repatriation). The choice of account affects repatriation of proceeds. **The Trap:** Investments CANNOT be made from overseas bank accounts directly — funds must be routed through Indian NRE/NRO accounts. This is a FEMA requirement, not just a mutual fund rule. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A covers NRI investment routing; Series II-B tests the RTA's validation of NRI payment instruments against authorized account types. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Advise NRI clients: NRE account investments → fully repatriable proceeds; NRO account investments → restricted repatriation (up to $1 million per year). Choose the account based on whether the client intends to take proceeds back abroad or keep them in India.
